items.title: Model a BOM as a header plus typed component lines
items.description: A bill of materials is a Bom header carrying the finished item it produces and the quantity produced per run, plus one or more BomLine rows each naming a component item and the quantity of that component consumed per run. The header-quantity-per-run is what lets a production order of any size be scaled deterministically against the recipe.
items.applicability: The recipe entity the generator emits before any production order; every material-issue and costing calculation reads from it.
items.example: CreateBom("Gadget from 2 Widgets", finishedItemId: Gadget, quantity: 1, lines: [(Widget, 2)]) yields a header that produces 1 Gadget and a single line consuming 2 Widgets per run.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: store the per-run quantity on both the header and each line so scaling is a single division/multiplication, never a hard-coded ratio. Test implication: assert a created BOM round-trips its finished item, header quantity and line quantities. Honest boundary: this is a single-level BOM only — no nested or multi-level component explosion is modelled.

items.title: A BOM must declare at least one component
items.description: BOM creation validates that the name is present, the per-run quantity is positive, every component line quantity is positive, and that at least one component line exists. An empty BOM is rejected with a domain exception because a recipe that consumes nothing cannot drive a material issue or a cost.
items.applicability: Domain-layer validation invoked whenever a BOM is authored, before it can back a production order.
items.example: CreateBom("Empty", finishedItemId, 1, lines: []) throws a DomainException; CreateBom with one positive-quantity component line succeeds.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: enforce the at-least-one-component and positive-quantity checks in the domain service, not only the UI, so the rule cannot be bypassed by an API caller. Test implication: assert an empty-component-list BOM throws. Honest boundary: validation covers structural well-formedness only; it does not verify that components are physically distinct from the finished item or that the recipe is economically sensible.

items.title: Create a production order from a BOM in Draft with a document number
items.description: Creating a production order from a BOM allocates a document number from the central numbering service, copies the finished item from the BOM, records the company, warehouse and quantity to produce, and starts the order in Draft status. The order quantity must be positive and the referenced BOM must exist.
items.applicability: The entry point of the manufacturing lifecycle; the order is the document every later transition mutates.
items.example: CreateOrder(company, warehouse, bomId, quantity: 10) returns an order with a non-empty DocNo and Status == Draft.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: derive the finished item from the BOM rather than re-supplying it, and obtain the DocNo from the shared numbering service so numbering stays consistent with other documents. Test implication: assert a new order is Draft with a non-empty DocNo and rejects a non-positive quantity. Honest boundary: there is no scheduling, planned start/end date, or work-centre assignment on the order.

items.title: Enforce a strict production-order lifecycle
items.description: A production order moves through Draft -> MaterialsIssued -> (QualityPassed | QualityFailed) -> Completed. Each transition checks the current status as a precondition: materials issue only from Draft, quality inspection only after materials are issued (or after a prior fail, to allow rework), and completion only from QualityPassed. Illegal transitions raise a domain exception.
items.applicability: The state machine that governs every manufacturing operation and keeps stock and costing in step with progress.
items.example: Calling Complete on an order that is still in MaterialsIssued (no quality pass yet) throws, whereas Draft -> issue -> pass -> complete succeeds.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: gate every transition method on the order's current status so steps cannot be skipped or reordered. Test implication: assert each out-of-order transition throws and the happy path advances status as expected. Honest boundary: the lifecycle has no partial-completion, sub-assembly, or operation-level progress states.

items.title: Scale component issue by the order-to-BOM run ratio
items.description: Issuing materials computes a run multiplier as order quantity divided by BOM per-run quantity, then for each component line the needed amount is the line quantity times that multiplier. Components are relieved from stock through the stock service so the stock ledger and valuation remain authoritative.
items.applicability: The consumption step that converts a recipe into actual stock movements for a specific order size.
items.example: A BOM that yields 1 finished unit from 2 components, on an order for 10 finished units, issues 20 components (runs = 10 / 1, needed = 2 * 10).
items.limitation: Implementation implication: relieve stock via the shared moving-average stock service (MoveOut), never by directly mutating quantity fields, so valuation stays correct. Test implication: assert issuing for a 10-unit order against a 2-per-unit recipe reduces component on-hand by exactly 20. Honest boundary: there is no scrap or yield-loss factor — issue is exactly the scaled recipe quantity.

items.title: Block material issue when any component is short
items.description: Before relieving any stock, the issue routine pre-checks every component: if the needed quantity exceeds on-hand for any single component, the whole issue is rejected with a domain exception and no stock is moved. This makes material issue all-or-nothing rather than leaving an order half-consumed.
items.applicability: The guard that prevents negative or oversold component stock during production.
items.example: With only 10 components on hand but 20 required, IssueMaterials throws and the component balance is unchanged.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate sufficiency for all lines in a first pass before performing any MoveOut, so a later shortage cannot leave earlier components already relieved. Test implication: assert an order needing more than on-hand throws and leaves stock untouched. Honest boundary: there is no automatic backorder, partial issue, or alternate-warehouse sourcing when short.

items.title: Accumulate material cost from issued-material valuation
items.description: As each component is issued, its cost is the needed quantity times that component's current moving-average valuation rate, summed across all lines and rounded, then stored on the order as MaterialCost. The cost therefore reflects the actual valuation of stock consumed, not a standard or catalogue price.
items.applicability: The costing step that turns physical consumption into a monetary production cost on the order.
items.example: Issuing 20 components valued at 60 each records MaterialCost = 1200 on the order.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: read the valuation rate from the same stock service that performs the MoveOut so cost and quantity relieved agree. Test implication: assert MaterialCost equals quantity-issued times valuation rate. Honest boundary: costing is material-only — no labour, machine, or overhead cost is captured.

items.title: Require an explicit quality inspection that can fail and be re-inspected
items.description: After materials are issued, an order must be inspected. A failing inspection sets QualityFailed and blocks completion; a passing inspection sets QualityPassed and allows it. A QualityFailed order may be re-inspected, so a fail followed by a later pass (rework) is a supported path to completion.
items.applicability: The quality gate that stands between consumption and finished-goods receipt.
items.example: Inspect(order, passed: false) then Complete throws; a subsequent Inspect(order, passed: true) then Complete succeeds and the order reaches Completed.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: allow the inspection method to run from both MaterialsIssued and QualityFailed states so rework is possible, and mark QualityInspected on the order. Test implication: assert a failed inspection blocks completion and a re-inspection pass permits it. Honest boundary: inspection is a single pass/fail flag — there is no sampling plan, defect quantity, scrap disposition, or partial accept/reject.

items.title: Complete only after a quality pass and receive the finished good at computed unit cost
items.description: Completion requires the order to be QualityPassed. On completion the unit cost is computed as MaterialCost divided by produced quantity (rounded), and the finished good is received into stock through the stock service at that unit cost, so the finished item's valuation inherits the production cost. The order then moves to Completed.
items.applicability: The step that turns a passed production order into on-hand, correctly valued finished goods.
items.example: MaterialCost 1200 over 10 units yields UnitCost 120; completing receives 10 finished units into the warehouse at 120 each.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: receive the finished good via the stock service (MoveIn) at the computed unit cost so finished-goods valuation reconciles with the material consumed. Test implication: assert completion adds the finished quantity to stock and sets unit cost to MaterialCost / quantity. Honest boundary: unit cost is purely material-derived; any WIP, labour or overhead absorption would change this figure and is not modelled.

items.title: A completed production order is immutable
items.description: Once an order reaches Completed, no further lifecycle operation is permitted: a second completion throws, and re-issuing materials throws because issue is only valid from Draft. This prevents double-receipt of finished goods or double-consumption of materials.
items.applicability: The terminal-state guard that protects the integrity of stock movements already posted.
items.example: After completing an order, both Complete and IssueMaterials called again each throw a domain exception.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat Completed as terminal in every mutating method, and detect it explicitly to give a clear error rather than a generic precondition failure. Test implication: assert repeat completion and re-issue both throw on a completed order. Honest boundary: there is no cancellation, reversal, or amendment workflow for a completed order — correction would require a compensating document not provided here.

items.title: Audit every production lifecycle transition
items.description: Each lifecycle action records an audit event against the production order: Create, IssueMaterials, QualityPassed or QualityFailed, and Complete. The audit trail captures who did what and when across the entire manufacturing flow, giving a reconstructable history independent of the order's current status.
items.applicability: Traceability and controls reporting over the manufacturing module.
items.example: Running a full Draft -> issue -> pass -> complete cycle leaves audit events for Create, IssueMaterials, QualityPassed and Complete on that ProductionOrder.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: record the audit event inside each transition method, alongside the status change and stock movement, so an action cannot succeed without leaving a trail. Test implication: assert the expected audit actions exist for a completed order. Honest boundary: the audit captures lifecycle actions and key figures only; it is not a full field-level change journal of every property.
